text: The surgeon begins dissection on the aortic plane, isolating and skeletonizing the inferior mesenteric artery while preserving the right autonomic nerves. The interfacial space is reached and dissected.

The surgeon isolates all branches of the inferior mesenteric artery and identifies the inferior mesenteric vein, which is then cut. The first sigmoid artery and superior rectal artery are divided, followed by a lymph node dissection.
The surgeon prepares the proximal and distal lines of resection on the colon, marking the areas for surgical division.

The surgeon ligates the colon with a wire ten centimeters below the tumor to facilitate rectal washout. The lumen is opened below the wire, and the anvil is gently inserted into the abdomen through the anus. Subsequently, the proximal colon lumen is opened, and the anvil is positioned for a side-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon performs resection of the proximal colon using a linear stapler, followed by completing the distal line of resection with a harmonic scalpel. A plastic sleeve is inserted through the rectum into the abdomen, and the specimen is extracted.
The surgeon closes the lumen of the distal line using a linear stapler, preparing the rectal stump for a side-to-end anastomosis with a circular stapler.

The surgeon performs a bubble test to check for leaks in the anastomosis, followed by the installation of a drainage tube into the pelvis. The video then shows the healed abdomen three months after the procedure, with no visible signs of complications.
